finding: High-level action identification (identifying action in terms of goals and consequences) covaries with mind attribution (attributing intentionality and cognition). Liking for target increases both identification level and mind attribution, with effect mediated by perspective-taking.

Acts of self-control reduce blood glucose levels. Low glucose after initial self-control predicts poor subsequent self-control performance. Glucose supplementation eliminates the impairment from prior self-control exertion.

People perceive themselves as less conforming to social influence than their peers. This asymmetry arises from weighting introspective information more heavily than behavioral evidence when assessing own conformity, while reversing this weighting for others.

Reducing relative left frontal cortical activation via neurofeedback decreases attitude change following decisions. Enhancing action-oriented processing increases left frontal activation and spreads attitudes more to support chosen alternative.

Victims express stronger need for power and empowerment; perpetrators express stronger need for social acceptance and understanding. Messages matching these differential needs increase willingness to reconcile more than mismatched messages.

Awareness of meaningful endings increases mixed emotions (simultaneous happiness and sadness). Effect occurs in both laboratory imagery and real-world situations (graduation), independent of chronological age.
